sections.label: Section 7.10
sections.original: 10 Ἐρεχθέος ἀστῶν , Ἄπολλον , οἳ τεόν γε δόμον
sections.translation: of the citizens of Erechtheus makes the rounds , Apollo , how they made your dwelling in divine Pytho a marvel to see . Five Isthmian victories lead my song forward , and one outstanding triumph

sections.label: Section 7.15
sections.original: Διὸς Ὀλυμπιάς ,
sections.translation: at Zeus ’ Olympian games , and two from Cirrha —

sections.label: Section 7.17
sections.original: ὦ Μεγάκλεες , ὑμαί τε καὶ προγόνων .
sections.translation: yours , Megacles , and your ancestors ’. I rejoice at this new success ; but I grieve that fine deeds are repaid with envy .

sections.label: Section 7.20
sections.original: 20 φαντί γε μὰν οὕτω κεν ἀνδρὶ παρμονίμαν
sections.translation: It is true what they say : the abiding bloom of good fortune brings with it both good and bad .

sections.label: Section 8.1
sections.original: φιλόφρον Ἡσυχία , Δίκας
sections.translation: Kindly Peace , daughter of Justice , you who make cities great , holding the supreme keys of counsels and of wars ,

sections.label: Section 8.5
sections.original: Πυθιόνικον τιμὰν Ἀριστομένει δέκευ .
sections.translation: receive this honor due to Aristomenes for his Pythian victory . For you know both how to give and how to receive gentleness , with precise timing .

sections.label: Section 8.8
sections.original: 10 τὺ δʼ , ὁπόταν τις ἀμείλιχον
sections.translation: And yet , whenever anyone drives pitiless anger into his heart ,

sections.label: Section 8.10
sections.original: τραχεῖα δυσμενέων
sections.translation: you meet the strength of your enemies roughly , sinking Arrogance in the flood . Porphyrion did not know your power , when he provoked you beyond all measure . Gain is most welcome , when one takes it from the home of a willing giver .

sections.label: Section 8.15
sections.original: 20 βία δὲ καὶ μεγάλαυχον ἔσφαλεν ἐν χρόνῳ .
sections.translation: Violence trips up even a man of great pride , in time . Cilician Typhon with his hundred heads did not escape you , nor indeed did the king of the Giants . [*] One was subdued by the thunderbolt , the other by the bow of Apollo , who with a gracious mind welcomed the son of Xenarces on his return from Cirrha , crowned with

sections.label: Section 8.20
sections.original: υἱὸν ποίᾳ Παρνασσίδι Δωριεῖ τε κώμῳ .
sections.translation: a garland of laurel from Parnassus and with Dorian victory-song .

sections.label: Section 8.21
sections.original: 30 ἔπεσε δʼ οὐ Χαρίτων ἑκὰς
sections.translation: His island with her just city has not fallen far from the Graces , having attained the famous excellence of the Aeacidae ; she has had perfect

sections.label: Section 8.25
sections.original: δόξαν ἀπʼ ἀρχᾶς . πολλοῖσι μὲν γὰρ ἀείδεται
sections.translation: glory from the beginning . She is praised in song for having fostered heroes who were supreme in many victory-bearing contests and in swift battles ;

sections.label: Section 8.28
sections.original: τὰ δὲ καὶ ἀνδράσιν ἐμπρέπει .
sections.translation: and she is distinguished in these things even for her men . But I do not have the time to set up

sections.label: Section 8.30
sections.original: πᾶσαν μακραγορίαν
sections.translation: their whole long story to the lyre and the gentle voice , for fear that satiety would come and distress us . But my debt to you , child , which comes running at my feet , your latest fine achievement , let it fly on the wings of my artfulness .

sections.label: Section 9.1
sections.original: ἐθέλω χαλκάσπιδα Πυθιονίκαν
sections.translation: With the help of the deep-waisted Graces I want to shout aloud proclaiming the Pythian victory with the bronze shield of Telesicrates , a prosperous man , the crowning glory of chariot-driving Cyrene ;

sections.label: Section 9.5
sections.original: τὰν ὁ χαιτάεις ἀνεμοσφαράγων ἐκ Παλίου κόλπων ποτὲ Λατοΐδας
sections.translation: the long-haired son of Leto once snatched her from the wind-echoing glens of Mt . Pelion , and carried the girl of the wilds in his golden chariot to a place where he made her mistress of a land rich in flocks and most rich in fruits , to live and flourish on the root of the third continent .

sections.label: Section 9.9
sections.original: ὑπέδεκτο δʼ ἀργυρόπεζʼ Ἀφροδίτα
sections.translation: Silver-footed Aphrodite welcomed

sections.label: Section 9.10
sections.original: Δάλιον ξεῖνον θεοδμάτων
sections.translation: the Delian guest from his chariot , touching him with a light hand , and she cast lovely modesty on their sweet union , joining together in a common bond of marriage the god and the daughter of wide-ruling Hypseus . He was at that time king of the proud Lapiths , a hero of the second generation from Oceanus ;

sections.label: Section 9.15
sections.original: δεύτερος · ὅν ποτε Πίνδου κλεενναῖς ἐν πτυχαῖς
sections.translation: in the renowned glens of Mt . Pindus a Naiad bore him , Creusa the daughter of Gaia , delighting in the bed of the river-god Peneius .

sections.label: Section 9.17
sections.original: Γαίας θυγάτηρ . ὁ δὲ τὰν εὐώλενον
sections.translation: And Hypseus raised his lovely-armed daughter Cyrene . She did not care for pacing back and forth at the loom , nor for the delights of luncheons with her stay-at-home companions ;

sections.label: Section 9.20
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἀκόντεσσίν τε χαλκέοις
sections.translation: instead , fighting with bronze javelins and with a sword , she killed wild beasts , providing great restful peace for her father ’ s cattle ; but as for her sweet bed-fellow , sleep ,

sections.label: Section 9.25
sections.original: ὕπνον ἀναλίσκοισα ῥέποντα πρὸς ἀῶ .
sections.translation: she spent only a little of it on her eyelids as it fell on them towards dawn .

sections.label: Section 9.26
sections.original: κίχε νιν λέοντί ποτʼ εὐρυφαρέτρας
sections.translation: Once the god of the broad quiver , Apollo who works from afar , came upon her wrestling alone and without spears with a terrible lion . Immediately he called Cheiron from out of his halls and spoke to him :

sections.label: Section 9.30
sections.original:
51
σεμνὸν ἄντρον , Φιλλυρίδα , προλιπὼν θυμὸν γυναικὸς καὶ μεγάλαν δύνασιν
sections.translation: “ Leave your sacred cave , son of Philyra , and marvel at the spirit and great strength of this woman ; look at what a struggle she is engaged in , with a fearless head , this young girl with a heart more than equal to any toil ; her mind is not shaken with the cold wind of fear . From what mortal was she born ? From what stock has this cutting been taken ,

sections.label: Section 10.1
sections.original: ὄλβια Λακεδαίμων ,
sections.translation: Lacedaemon is prosperous ; Thessaly is divinely blessed . Both are ruled by the race of a single ancestor , Heracles , the best in battle . Why do I make this untimely boast ? Because Pytho summons me , and Pelinna ,

sections.label: Section 10.5
sections.original: Ἀλεύα τε παῖδες , Ἱπποκλέᾳ ἐθέλοντες
sections.translation: and the sons of Aleuas ; they want me to present to Hippocleas the glorious voices of men in celebration .

sections.label: Section 10.7
sections.original: γεύεται γὰρ ἀέθλων ·
sections.translation: For he is trying his hand at contests , and the gorge of Parnassus proclaimed him to the people that live around as the greatest of the boys in the double-course footrace .

sections.label: Section 10.10
sections.original: Ἄπολλον , γλυκὺ δʼ ἀνθρώπων τέλος ἀρχά τε δαίμονος ὀρνύντος αὔξεται ·
sections.translation: Apollo , the end and the beginning both grow sweet when a god urges on a man « s work . No doubt he accomplished this with the help of your counsels . Kinship has stepped into the footprints of the father ,

sections.label: Section 10.13
sections.original: Ὀλυμπιονίκα δὶς ἐν πολεμαδόκοις
sections.translation: who was twice an Olympic victor in the war-enduring armor of Ares ;

sections.label: Section 10.15
sections.original: ἔθηκε καὶ βαθυλείμων ὑπὸ Κίρρας ἀγὼν
sections.translation: and the contest in the deep meadow that stretches beneath the rock of Cirrha made Phricias victorious in the race . May a good fate follow them in their future days as well , so that their noble wealth will blossom ;

sections.label: Section 10.20
sections.original: 30 λαχόντες οὐκ ὀλίγαν δόσιν , μὴ φθονεραῖς ἐκ θεῶν
sections.translation: having received no small share of the delights of Greece , may they encounter no envious reversals at the hands of the gods . A god « s heart should be free from pain ; but a man is considered fortunate , and wise poets sing his praises , if he wins victory with his hands or the excellence of his feet , and takes the greatest prizes through his courage and strength ,

sections.label: Section 10.25
sections.original: καὶ ζώων ἔτι νεαρὸν
sections.translation: and lives to see his young son duly winning Pythian garlands . He can never set foot in the bronze heavens ; but whatever splendor we mortals can attain , he reaches the limit of that voyage . Neither by ship nor on foot could you find

sections.label: Section 10.30
sections.original: ἐς Ὑπερβορέων ἀγῶνα θαυματὰν ὁδόν .
sections.translation: the marvellous road to the meeting-place of the Hyperboreans —

sections.label: Section 11.1
sections.original: Κάδμου κόραι , Σεμέλα μὲν Ὀλυμπιάδων ἀγυιᾶτις ,
sections.translation: [*] Daughters of Cadmus , Semele dwelling among the Olympians and Ino Leucothea , sharing the chamber of the Nereid sea-nymphs : come , with the mother of Heracles , greatest in birth , to the presence of Melia ; come to the sanctuary of golden tripods ,

sections.label: Section 11.5
sections.original: θησαυρόν , ὃν περίαλλʼ ἐτίμασε Λοξίας ,
sections.translation: the treasure-house which Loxias honored above all

sections.label: Section 11.6
sections.original: 10 Ἰσμήνιον δʼ ὀνύμαξεν , ἀλαθέα μαντίων θῶκον ,
sections.translation: and named the Ismenion , true seat of prophecy . Come , children of Harmonia , where even now he calls the native host of heroines to assemble , so that you may loudly sing of holy Themis and Pytho and the just

sections.label: Section 11.10
sections.original: γᾶς ὀμφαλὸν κελαδήσετʼ ἄκρᾳ σὺν ἑσπέρᾳ ,
sections.translation: navel of the earth , at the edge of evening ,

sections.label: Section 11.11
sections.original: ἑπταπύλοισι Θήβαις
sections.translation: in honor of seven-gated Thebes and the contest at Cirrha , in which Thrasydaeus caused his ancestral hearth to be remembered by flinging over it a third wreath

sections.label: Section 11.15
sections.original: ἐν ἀφνεαῖς ἀρούραισι Πυλάδα
sections.translation: as a victor in the rich fields of Pylades , the friend of Laconian Orestes ,

sections.label: Section 11.17
sections.original: τὸν δὴ φονευομένου πατρὸς Ἀρσινόα Κλυταιμνήστρας
sections.translation: who indeed , when his father was murdered , was taken by his nurse Arsinoe from the strong hands and bitter deceit of Clytaemnestra , when she sent the Dardanian daughter of Priam ,

sections.label: Section 11.20
sections.original: 30 Κασσάνδραν πολιῷ χαλκῷ σὺν Ἀγαμεμνονίᾳ
sections.translation: Cassandra , together with the soul of Agamemnon , to the shadowy bank of Acheron with her gray blade of bronze ,

sections.label: Section 11.22
sections.original: νηλὴς γυνά . πότερόν νιν ἄρʼ Ἰφιγένειʼ ἐπʼ Εὐρίπῳ
sections.translation: the pitiless woman . Was it Iphigeneia , slaughtered at the Euripus far from her fatherland , that provoked her to raise the heavy hand of her anger ? Or was she vanquished by another bed

sections.label: Section 11.25
sections.original: 40 ἔννυχοι πάραγον κοῖται ; τὸ δὲ νέαις ἀλόχοις
sections.translation: and led astray by their nightly sleeping together ? This is the most hateful error for young brides , and is impossible to conceal

sections.label: Section 11.27
sections.original: ἀλλοτρίαισι γλώσσαις ·
sections.translation: because other people will talk . Citizens are apt to speak evil , for prosperity brings with it envy as great as itself .

sections.label: Section 11.30
sections.original: ὁ δὲ χαμηλὰ πνέων ἄφαντον βρέμει .
sections.translation: But the man who breathes close to the ground roars unseen . He himself died , the heroic son of Atreus , when at last he returned to famous Amyclae ,

sections.label: Section 12.1
sections.original: αἰτέω σε , φιλάγλαε , καλλίστα βροτεᾶν πολίων ,
sections.translation: I beseech you , splendor-loving city , most beautiful on earth , home of Persephone ; you who inhabit the hill of well-built dwellings above the banks of sheep-pasturing Acragas : be propitious , and with the goodwill of gods and men , mistress ,

sections.label: Section 12.5
sections.original: 10 δέξαι στεφάνωμα τόδʼ ἐκ Πυθῶνος εὐδόξῳ Μίδᾳ ,
sections.translation: receive this victory garland from Pytho in honor of renowned Midas , and receive the victor himself , champion of Hellas in that art which once Pallas Athena discovered when she wove into music the dire dirge of the reckless Gorgons

sections.label: Section 12.9
sections.original: τὸν παρθενίοις ὑπό τʼ ἀπλάτοις ὀφίων κεφαλαῖς
sections.translation: which Perseus heard

sections.label: Section 12.10
sections.original: ἄϊε λειβόμενον δυσπενθέϊ σὺν καμάτῳ ,
sections.translation: pouring in slow anguish from beneath the horrible snakey hair of the maidens , when he did away with the third sister and brought death to sea-girt Seriphus and its people . Yes , he brought darkness on the monstrous race of Phorcus , and he repaid Polydectes with a deadly wedding-present for the long

sections.label: Section 12.15
sections.original: δουλοσύναν τό τʼ ἀναγκαῖον λέχος ,
sections.translation: slavery of his mother and her forced bridal bed ; he stripped off the head of beautiful Medusa ,

sections.label: Section 12.17
sections.original: 30 υἱὸς Δανάας · τὸν ἀπὸ χρυσοῦ φαμεν αὐτορύτου
sections.translation: Perseus , the son of Danae , who they say was conceived in a spontaneous shower of gold . But when the virgin goddess had released that beloved man from those labors , she created the many-voiced song of flutes

sections.label: Section 12.20
sections.original: ὄφρα τὸν Εὐρυάλας ἐκ καρπαλιμᾶν γενύων
sections.translation: so that she could imitate with musical instruments the shrill cry that reached her ears from the fast-moving jaws of Euryale . The goddess discovered it ; but she discovered it for mortal men to have , and called it the many-headed strain , the glorious strain that entices the people to gather at contests ,

sections.label: Section 12.25
sections.original: λεπτοῦ διανισσόμενον χαλκοῦ θαμὰ καὶ δονάκων ,
sections.translation: often sounding through thin plates of brass and through reeds , which grow beside the city of lovely choruses , the city of the Graces , in the sacred precinct of the nymph of Cephisus , reeds that are the faithful witnesses of the dancers . If there is any prosperity among men , it does not appear without hardship . A god will indeed grant it in full today . . .

sections.label: Section 12.30
sections.original: δαίμων — τὸ δὲ μόρσιμον οὐ παρφυκτόν ,— ἀλλʼ ἔσται χρόνος
sections.translation: What is fated cannot be escaped . But that time will come , striking unexpectedly , and give one thing beyond all expectation , and withhold another .
